title: Open the banking app, exhale, and close it
subtitle: A graduated daily activity for reducing the dread of checking a balance.
excerpt: A seven-day graduated activity for balance avoidance. The task is small: open the banking app once per day, let the balance load, take one slow exhale, and close it. No reviewing, no scrolling, no calculating. One minute. The goal is to build tolerance to the sight of the number before anything else.
sourceNotes: Graduated exposure as a technique for reducing avoidance behaviour is documented in cognitive-behavioural therapy research. The money-avoidance application is adapted from Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025), "Stop obsessing over debt."

content.text: A seven-day graduated activity for balance avoidance. The task each day is small: open the banking app, let the balance load, take one slow exhale, and close it. No reviewing, no scrolling, no calculating what the numbers mean today. One minute. The goal is to build tolerance to the sight of the number before asking anything more from the session.

Once per day, at a consistent time (same time each day works better than ad hoc): pick up the phone, open the banking app, navigate to the main balance. Watch the number load. Take one slow exhale, in through the nose, out slowly through a slightly open mouth. Then close the app. The session is done.
Don't scroll transactions. Don't calculate what's due. Don't open a second account screen. One balance, one exhale, close.
Say the affirmation once before you open the app each day:

attrs.quote: I look at my balance from steady ground.

content.text: Seven days, one minute per day. The task stays the same throughout. By day four or five, the opening of the app typically carries less spike than it did on day one. The nervous system is learning that opening the app does not require emergency. That's the only goal of this practice: lower the spike.

If the spike is still high on day seven, run the practice for a second week before moving to a longer session. The tolerance has to be in place before the more detailed work (reviewing transactions, making a plan) is useful.
If the dread of opening the app is too high to start even this small task, tap first.
lowers the emotional charge before the graduated exposure begins.

Graduated exposure is a known way to lower avoidance, used in CBT work. Adapted here for money, from Day 6 of MONEY: A 12-Week Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025). Day 6 covers the avoid-then-worry loop that keeps debt fears strong.
